title: इन्द्र द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री इन्द्र द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम् ॥

प्रथमं इन्द्रमित्युक्तं द्वितीयं शक्रमेव च।
तृतीयं पुरन्दरं चैव चतुर्थं वज्रपाणिनम्॥१॥

पञ्चमं देवराजं च षष्ठं सहस्राक्षं विदुः।
सप्तमं मेघवाहनं अष्टमं शचीपतिं तथा॥२॥

नवमं जम्भभेदिनं दशमं वृत्रहन्तरम्।
एकादशं सुरपतिं द्वादशं स्वर्गपालकम्॥३॥

द्वादशैतानि नामानि इन्द्रदेवस्य कीर्तयेत्।
यः पठेत् प्रातरुत्थाय सर्वसम्पत्तिमाप्नुयात्॥४॥

वर्षा सुकालं जायेत कृषिफलं समृद्धिमत्।
इन्द्रप्रसादतः सर्वं ऐश्वर्यं समवाप्यते॥५॥

॥ इति श्री इन्द्र द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Indra Dwadash Nama Stotram ||

Prathamam Indram-ityuktam Dvitiyam Shakram Eva Cha.
Tritiyam Purandaram Chaiva Chaturtham Vajra-paninam.||1||

Panchamam Deva-rajam Cha Shashtham Sahasra-aksham Viduh.
Saptamam Megha-vahanam Ashtamam Shachi-patim Tatha.||2||

Navamam Jambha-bhedinam Dashamam Vritra-hantaram.
Ekadasham Sura-patim Dvadasham Svarga-palakam.||3||

Dvadashai-tani Namani Indra-devasya Kirtayet.
Yah Pathet Pratar-utthaya Sarva-sampattim-apnuyat.||4||

Varsha Sukalam Jayeta Krishi-phalam Samriddhimat.
Indra-prasadatah Sarvam Aishvaryam Samavapyate.||5||

|| Iti Shri Indra Dwadash Nama Stotram Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning: प्रथम नाम इन्द्र, दूसरा शक्र, तीसरा पुरन्दर, चौथा वज्रपाणि। पाँचवाँ देवराज, छठा सहस्राक्ष, सातवाँ मेघवाहन, आठवाँ शचीपति। नवाँ जम्भभेदी, दसवाँ वृत्रहन्ता, ग्यारहवाँ सुरपति, बारहवाँ स्वर्गपालक। इन्द्रदेव के इन बारह नामों को प्रातःकाल पढ़ने से सर्वसम्पत्ति प्राप्त होती है, वर्षा सुकाल होती है, कृषिफल समृद्ध होता है।
benefits: Twelve Vedic names of Lord Indra, king of the devas
Invokes all forms of wealth and prosperity
Traditionally recited to invoke timely rainfall and agricultural abundance
Invokes the blessing of aisvarya (lordship) and power by indra's grace
Vedic prayer for protection and cosmic order
description: Indra Dwadash Nama Stotram — इन्द्र द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम् — is a devotional hymn of praise offered to Lord Indra. This sacred composition is sourced from Rig Veda and continues to inspire devotees worldwide. Devotees recite this prayer to bestow aisvarya (lordship) and power by Indra's grace and twelve Vedic names of Lord Indra, king of the devas. It is also said to ensure timely rainfall and agricultural abundance. It is especially recommended on Thursday (Guruvar) and Sunday (Ravivar) for maximum spiritual benefit. Comprising 5 verses, it can be completed in approximately 3 minutes, making it suitable for daily devotional practice. On this page, you can read the complete Indra Dwadash Nama Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Indra Dwadash Nama Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: The Indra Dwadash Nama Stotram (इन्द्र द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम्) is a short 5-verse Sanskrit composition that lists the 12 (dvadasha) most sacred names of Lord Indra — the Vedic king of the devas, ruler of Svarga (heaven), wielder of the vajra (thunderbolt) weapon, and the most-invoked deity of the Rigveda (over 250 hymns dedicated to Him). The 12 names are: (1) Indra — the chief; (2) Shakra — the mighty; (3) Purandara — destroyer of demon-cities; (4) Vajra-pani — thunderbolt-handed; (5) Deva-raja — king of devas; (6) Sahasra-aksha — thousand-eyed; (7) Megha-vahana — cloud-rider; (8) Shachi-pati — consort of Shachi (Indrani); (9) Jambha-bhedi — slayer of demon Jambha; (10) Vritra-hantra — slayer of Vritra; (11) Sura-pati — lord of devas/suras; (12) Svarga-palaka — protector of Svarga. The stotra is preserved in classical Vedic-Smriti tradition. (Source: Rig Veda / Vedic dvadasha-nama tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is Indra called the king of devas in Vedic religion?
seoFaqs.answer: Indra holds the supreme position in Vedic religion (above even Brahma-Vishnu-Shiva who rose to prominence in later Puranic times) for several reasons: (1) The Rigveda dedicates 250+ hymns to Indra — more than any other deity — making Him quantitatively the most-praised god in the world's oldest religious text; (2) He defeated the cosmic-dragon Vritra (drought-personified) by hurling the vajra weapon — releasing the cosmic waters and enabling life on earth; (3) He destroyed Vritra's mother Danu, the demon Jambha, the demon Namuchi, and the 99 forts of demon Shambara — earning the title Purandara (city-destroyer); (4) He leads the devas in all asura-deva wars; (5) He is the cosmic-rain god whose timely-rainfall enables agriculture; (6) His thousand-eyes (Sahasra-aksha) symbolize omniscience; (7) He rides the divine elephant Airavata, holds the vajra, and drinks Soma; (8) His city Amaravati in Svarga is the destination of all righteous souls. (Sources: Rigveda; Aitareya Brahmana; Mahabharata Sabha Parva.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of each of Indra's 12 sacred names?
seoFaqs.answer: Each name reveals one aspect: (1) Indra — from idi (cleaving/conquering) — the conqueror; (2) Shakra — from shak (capable) — the all-capable; (3) Purandara — destroyer (dara) of cities (pura) — the demolisher of demon-fortresses (Tripura too); (4) Vajra-pani — holder of the vajra in his hand, made from the bones of Sage Dadhichi for slaying Vritra; (5) Deva-raja — supreme king of all 33 (or 330 million) devas; (6) Sahasra-aksha — having thousand-eyes (originally Gautama-rishi cursed Indra to have 1000 vulvas-marks for seducing Ahalya, later transformed to 1000 eyes by Brahma's blessing); (7) Megha-vahana — riding the clouds (Indra is the rain-god); (8) Shachi-pati — consort of Shachi-Devi (Indrani — daughter of asura Puloma); (9) Jambha-bhedi — slayer of asura Jambha (yawning-demon of paralysis); (10) Vritra-hantra — slayer of Vritra (the most famous Vedic deed); (11) Sura-pati — lord of the suras (devas); (12) Svarga-palaka — protector of Svarga where righteous souls go. (Sources: Rigveda; Nirukta of Yaska; Brihaddevata.)

seoFaqs.question: When should the Indra Dwadash Nama be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The Indra Dwadash Nama is most powerfully recited on Thursday (Guruvar — day of Brihaspati who is Indra's guru and priest), Sunday (Ravivar — to honor Surya as Indra's brother in the Aditya group), during Indra-dhwaja festival (Bhadrapada Shukla Dvadashi — when royal flagstaffs are raised in Indra's honor); during Govardhan Puja day after Diwali (when Krishna lifted Govardhan to humble Indra — so reciting Indra's namavali appeases Him); during agricultural rain-invocation rituals (since Indra is the rain-god); during drought; before any vrishti-yajna (rain-sacrifice); during Karkataka Sankranti (Cancer transit when monsoon arrives); during Shravan and Bhadra months (peak monsoon time); for those facing deva-shapa (curses from devas) or who have lost their position/authority (since Indra Himself fell from his throne multiple times and recovered through devotion); for warriors and kshatriyas seeking valor. (Sources: Vedic ritual paddhati; Rajasuya yajna procedures.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does the Indra Dwadash Nama promise?
seoFaqs.answer: Verses 4-5 of the stotra — the phala-shruti — promise specific benefits: (1) Yah pathet pratar-utthaya sarva-sampattim apnuyat — one who recites at sunrise gains all forms of wealth; (2) Varsha sukalam jayeta — timely and good rainfall is granted; (3) Krishi-phalam samriddhi-mat — agricultural produce becomes abundant; (4) Indra-prasadatah sarvam aishvaryam samavapyate — by Indra's grace all aishvarya (lordship/sovereignty/wealth/power) is obtained. Additionally, traditional Indra-upasana grants: leadership and authority (since Indra is Deva-raja), victory in conflicts (since Indra is Vritra-hantra), protection from natural disasters especially drought-floods-storms (since Indra controls weather), success in agriculture and dairy farming, attainment of Svarga-loka after death (since Indra is Svarga-palaka), kingship and royal-position (Indra was the model for Vedic-period kingship), and removal of obstacles in career advancement. The 12-name namavali grants all these in concise daily-recitation. (Sources: phala-shruti verses 4-5; Vedic Indra-stuti tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: How is Indra worshipped in modern Hindu practice despite Puranic decline?
seoFaqs.answer: While Indra's prominence declined in Puranic times (overshadowed by Vishnu, Shiva, Devi worship), He continues to be worshipped through specific traditions: (1) Indra-dhwaja festival in Bhadrapada Shukla Dvadashi — royal flagstaffs in Nepal (Indra Jatra of Kathmandu — major festival), Manipur, and erstwhile princely states; (2) Govardhan Puja / Annakut on Kartik Shukla Pratipada — though commemorating Krishna's victory over Indra, devotees also offer prayers to Indra to maintain timely rainfall; (3) Karkataka Sankranti monsoon-arrival prayers; (4) Vedic Sandhya Vandana — every Brahmin daily invokes Indra in the Gayatri-mantra recitation and arghya offering; (5) all Vedic yajnas (Soma yajna, Ashvamedha, Rajasuya, Vajapeya) center on Indra-worship; (6) before any battle or competition by warriors and athletes; (7) as one of the 8 Dik-palakas (East-direction guardian) in Vastu Shastra and temple-architecture; (8) in agricultural communities of Bihar, Tamil Nadu, and Karnataka where rain-prayers continue; (9) in Buddhism and Jainism Indra (as Shakra/Sakka) remains supreme heaven-king. The Dwadash Nama maintains continuity of His worship. (Sources: Indra Jatra Nepal; Vedic-Brahmin daily ritual; Vastu Shastra.)
